subject: Gastroenterology
title: Esophageal perforation
preambleMarkdown:
# Esophageal perforation



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Esophageal perforation is a clinical condition or syndrome in gastroenterology.
- This article focuses on GI anatomy, motility, inflammation, bleeding, obstruction, liver-pancreas-biliary disease, and nutrition.
- The practical clinical priority is gastroenterology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Esophageal perforation to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Esophageal perforation is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is gastroenterology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Esophageal perforation depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In gastroenterology, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Esophageal perforation.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Esophageal perforation disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Esophageal perforation should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Esophageal perforation first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Esophageal perforation, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Esophageal perforation, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused gastroenterology examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Esophageal perforation presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Esophageal perforation with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Esophageal perforation is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Esophageal perforation should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Esophageal perforation is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses gastroenterology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Esophageal perforation, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Esophageal perforation may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Esophageal perforation patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Esophageal perforation, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Esophageal perforation in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Esophageal perforation, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Esophageal perforation does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Esophageal perforation may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Esophageal perforation, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Esophageal perforation as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Esophageal perforation draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

### Source Excerpt: Esophageal Perforation

_Source lines: 14167-14208._

```text
Esophageal Perforation
- Etiology: blunt trauma, penetrating trauma, iatrogenic injury
(endoscopy, cardiac ablation, etc.), forceful vomiting (Boerhaave

syndrome).

- Clinical features: pain (severe retrosternal chest, neck, epigastric or
shoulder pain), odynophagia, dyspnea, signs of sepsis (fever,
tachypnea, tachycardia, hypotension), Hamman sign ("mediastinal
crunch" produced by the heart beating against air-filled tissues),
pneumothorax, or pleural effusion.

- Contrast esophagram is definitive diagnostic study (soluble
Gastrografin swallow preferred).

- CXR usually shows air in the mediastinum.

- CT of chest and abdomen if unable to obtain contrast esophagram and
to look for fluid collections requiring drainage
- If the patient is stable and the perforation is small (draining into
lumen), medical management is appropriate: IV fluids, NPO,
antibiotics, and PPI.

- If patient is ill and the perforation is large (or if there is
communication into pleural cavity), surgery should be performed
within 24 hours of presentation (success rate is higher)
Quick HIT
The time interval between esophageal perforation and surgery is
the most important factor in determining survival. If surgery is
delayed beyond 24 hours, the mortality rate and the likelihood
of complications increase.

Quick HIT
Most cases of PUD are due to H. pylori infection and NSAID use. It
can be dificult to determine the cause in a patient with H. pylori
infection who also uses NSAIDs. Both may be responsible.

Therefore, if in doubt, test for H. pylori.

Diseases of the Stomach
```

<!-- source: lib.md import end -->

### Source Excerpt: Esophageal Disorders

_Source lines: 8872-9247._

```text
II. ESOPHAGEAL DISORDERS
Esophageal problems present with one of three predominant symptoms: pain, odyno-
phagia/dysphagia, or bleeding.

A. Esophageal pain

## 1. GERD

a. Discussion
i. Incidence. Ten percent of the population experiences reflux symptoms
daily, and 33% experience symptoms at least once a month. Reflux oc-
curs in 70% of pregnant women and is very common in infants.

ii. Pathogenesis. The reflux is caused by a decrease in the lower esopha-
geal sphincter pressure. Pregnancy and drugs that cause smooth muscle
relaxation (e.g., " agonists, peppermint, calcium channel blockers) may
augment lower esophageal sphincter pressure. Physical conditions such
as mid-body obesity or hiatal hernia may promote relaxation.

b. Clinical features. The reflux of acidic gastric contents into the esophagus
causes a retrosternal, burning pain that may radiate to the neck or jaw.

More than 50% of "chest pain" seen in the ED may be reflux.

i. The pain is often exacerbated by activities that overcome the lower
esophageal sphincter resting pressure such as bending over, lying down,
or consuming large meals.

ii. Uncomplicated GERD is classically relieved within minutes by sitting
up and drinking liquids or taking antacids.

c. Differential diagnoses. Pain from GERD can be indistinguishable from chest pain
of cardiac origin; therefore, a cardiac cause must be ruled out. The resolution of
symptoms after a trial of oral antacids in the ED does not necessarily exclude a
cardiac cause because many patients have both cardiac disease and GERD.

d. Evaluation is primarily done on an outpatient basis and may include an
upper gastrointestinal series, ambulatory acid reflux testing, endoscopy, or
manometry to look for spasm as the cause of pain.

e. Therapy entails the following supportive measures:
i. Elevating the head of the bed 6 inches (or 30 degrees) and avoiding re-
cumbency within 3 hours of eating
ii. Eating smaller meals and avoiding fatty foods, chocolate, caffeine, pep-
permint, or alcohol
iii. Smoking cessation
iv. Weight reduction (in obese patients)
v. Over-the-counter antacids, histamine-2 antagonists (e.g., ranitidine,
cimetidine), or other agents such as proton pump inhibitors (PPIs; e.g.,
omeprazole, pantoprazole) are available for short-term use.

f. Disposition. Patients at risk for coronary disease should be admitted. If a
cardiac cause can be excluded, the patient may be safely discharged and fur-
ther evaluated as an outpatient.

## 2. Esophagitis

a. Discussion
i. Infectious esophagitis occurs more commonly in immunocompro-
mised hosts (e.g., patients with AIDS, patients with diabetes mellitus,
patients receiving chemotherapy, organ transplant patients). Commonly
implicated organisms include Candida and herpes simplex virus and
cytomegalovirus (CMV).

Quick 	HIT
Humane application of anal-
gesia should not be withheld
from any patient without
good cause.

Quick 	HIT
Therefore, although antacids
are often useful to relieve
pain that is esophageal in
origin, the effect is insuffi-
ciently specific to be of value
in aiding diagnosis.

G A S T R O I N T E S T I N A L E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	111

Gastrointestinal Emergencies
ii. Pill esophagitis occurs after taking caustic oral medications. The tablet
adheres to the esophageal wall and dissolves, leading to local inflam-
mation and irritation. Medications frequently responsible include doxy-
cycline (the most common offender), potassium chloride, quinidine,
NSAIDs, ferrous sulfate, and oral bisphosphonates.

b. Clinical features. Infectious or pill esophagitis presents as odynophagia
(pain with swallowing). Unlike GERD pain, odynophagia predominates.

Often the patients will drool or spit to avoid swallowing. Anorexia is
common.

i. Candida esophagitis. Pain may be mild or severe. Oral candidiasis is
often present (especially in patients with AIDS).

ii. Herpes or CMV esophagitis. The onset of pain is typically abrupt, and
there may be a history of recent upper respiratory tract infection. Herpes
or CMV esophagitis is associated with ulcerative lesions (distinct from
the heaped plaques associated with oral candidiasis). Immunocompetent
patients frequently demonstrate skin or oral lesions, but immunocom-
promised patients often do not.

c. Differential diagnoses
i. Cardiac disease must always be excluded.

ii. Oropharyngeal odynophagia can be caused by pharyngitis, retropha-
ryngeal abscess, peritonsillar abscess, or epiglottitis and must be distin-
guished from esophageal odynophagia.

d. Evaluation
i. Candida esophagitis may be confirmed in the ED using an air-contrast
barium swallow, which will reveal ulceration and plaques.

ii. Evaluation should include a soft tissue film of the neck if a retropha-
ryngeal abscess or epiglottitis is suspected.

iii. Esophagoscopy with biopsy and culture is the definitive study.

e. Therapy
i. Candida esophagitis. Treatment is with oral ketoconazole or flucon-
azole. Granulocytopenic patients are at risk for dissemination; there-
fore, treatment with intravenous amphotericin B is warranted for these
patients.

ii. Herpes esophagitis is self-limited in immunocompetent patients. Treat-
ment with oral acyclovir may shorten healing time. Oral or intravenous
acyclovir may be required for immunocompromised patients.

iii. Pill esophagitis. Symptoms usually resolve without any specific
treatment. The offending medication should be discontinued if pos-
sible. If it is not possible to discontinue the medication, the patient
should take the pills with plenty of water and remain in an upright
position (sitting or standing) for 10 to 30 minutes immediately
after taking the medication. A short course of antacids or acid-
suppressing medications will provide symptom relief until the ulcer
has healed.

f. Disposition. Immunocompetent patients with mild symptoms may be em-
pirically treated with oral fluconazole and should be referred for outpatient
follow-up within a week. Patients who are unable to maintain adequate
oral fluid intake or those at risk for disseminated infection should be
admitted.

3. Boerhaave syndrome is rupture of the esophagus during forceful emesis.

a. Clinical features. The patient reports an episode of severe, violent emesis
and complains of severe chest pain with or without shortness of air.

b. Evaluation
i. Physical examination
a) Hamman sign is a crunching sound heard on auscultation of the
heart. The crunching sound is due to mediastinal emphysema.

b) Crepitation may be found in the neck and signifies subcutaneous
emphysema.

112 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Gastrointestinal Emergencies
c) Signs of septic shock, fever, hypotension, or altered mentation may
be seen in late presentations.

ii. Diagnostic imaging
a) Chest radiographs may demonstrate mediastinal air, subcutaneous em-
physema, a widened mediastinum, pneumothorax, or air-fluid levels.

b) Water-soluble contrast swallow will show extravasation.

c. Therapy entails fluid resuscitation, antibiotics, and emergent surgical repair.

A chest tube may be needed if a pneumothorax is present.

4. Motility disorders (see II.B.1.b and II.B.2.b) may produce esophageal pain.

B. Esophageal dysphagia
1. Discussion. Dysphagia is defined as difficulty swallowing. It may or may not
be accompanied by pain. Causes of esophageal dysphagia include:
a. Obstructive disorders (e.g., aortic aneurysm, carcinoma, webs, and rings)
b. Motor disorders (e.g., achalasia, diffuse esophageal spasm, and scleroderma)
c. Acute food impaction (typically meat)
2. Clinical features
a. Obstructive disorders. Patients tend to have greater dysphagia for solids
than for liquids.

b. Motor disorders. Patients tend to have equal dysphagia for both solids
and liquids. Dysphagia resulting from a motor disorder is often associated
with pain.

c. Food impaction often occurs in social situations and is often associated
with alcohol. It may or may not indicate underlying esophageal disorders.

3. Differential diagnoses
a. Cardiac disorders must be excluded if the patient is experiencing chest pain.

b. Oropharyngeal dysphagia is defined as difficulty passing food from the mouth
to the esophagus and must be differentiated from esophageal dysphagia.

i. Common causes include cerebrovascular accident, multiple sclerosis,
myasthenia gravis, and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis.

ii. Clinical features may include drooling, coughing, choking, nasal regur-
gitation, or aspiration. Patients with an acute sensation that something
is stuck or who are unable to handle secretions most often have acute
food impaction.

## 4. Evaluation

a. Radiography. A chest radiograph may demonstrate an aortic aneurysm,
a tumor, a foreign body, or a dilated esophagus with an air-fluid level
(characteristic of achalasia).

b. Other studies are carried out on an outpatient basis for the nonemergent patient
and may include endoscopy, a barium swallow, manometry, and motility studies.

## 5. Therapy

a. Supportive therapy. Intravenous hydration may be necessary.

b. Definitive therapy
i. Obstructive disorders
a) Medical treatment. Acute esophageal obstruction may be treated med-
ically with glucagon, calcium channel blockers (e.g., nifedipine) to in-
duce smooth muscle relaxation, and antispasmodics (e.g., diazepam).

b) Acute endoscopy is indicated if medical treatment fails to resolve
the problem and complete esophageal obstruction persists. In these
situations, endoscopy should be performed as soon as possible to
avoid complications (perforation, mucosal necrosis, etc.).

ii. Motility disorders (spasm) may be treated with nitrates, calcium chan-
nel blockers, or anticholinergic drugs to reduce symptoms pending
definitive diagnosis
6. Disposition. Most patients can be discharged for an outpatient work up.

Dehydration or complete esophageal obstruction are the major indications
for admission.

C. Esophageal bleeding. An esophageal source is responsible for approximately 25%
of cases of upper gastrointestinal tract bleeding. Of these, nearly 80% are due
Quick 	HIT
It is crucial to avoid barium
studies in patients sus-
pected of having complete
esophageal obstruction as
barium may preclude the fea-
sibility of urgent endoscopy.

G A S T R O I N T E S T I N A L E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	113

Gastrointestinal Emergencies
to esophageal varices and 20% are due to Mallory-Weiss tears or severe erosive
esophagitis.

1. Esophageal varices
a. Discussion. Varices are dilated submucosal veins that result from portal hy-
pertension, which is most commonly caused by cirrhosis of the liver.

b. Clinical features. Patients with esophageal varices commonly present with
painless and massive hematemesis. The stigmata of chronic liver disease
(e.g., spider angiomas, palmar erythema, ascites, gynecomastia, and testicu-
lar atrophy) may be present as well.

c. Therapy
i. Supportive therapy. Measures include:
a) Administration of intravenous fluids through two large-bore intra-
venous lines
b) Administration of packed red blood cells (RBCs) to a target hemo-
globin of 7 gm/dL. Over-transfusion is associated with rebleeding or
continuous bleeding due to increased portal pressure and should be
avoided.

c) Fresh frozen plasma, platelet infusion, or recombinant factor VII
may be administered to correct any coagulopathy.

d) Nasogastric suction to monitor bleeding is controversial and can
provide misleading information.

e) Octreotide, a synthetic somatostatin compound indicated for ter-
minating upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage while not inducing
vasoconstriction.

1) Dose. Octreotide is administered as an intravenous infusion at
a rate of 50 #g/hour for 72 hours or longer, which reduces re-
bleeding, transfusion requirements, and improves mortality.

2) Complications of octreotide include hypoglycemia, hyperglyce-
mia, and thrombocytopenia.

f) The use of ceftriaxone 1 gm intravenously daily or norfloxacin
400 mg daily for 7 days significantly reduces infectious sequelae
and reduces portal pressure via gut decontamination reduction of
endotoxemia.

g) Erythromycin 250 to 500 mg given 30 minutes prior to endoscopy
may aid in visualization by promoting gastric motility and empty-
ing blood from the stomach.

h) Intravenous PPI reduces transfusion requirements and helps stop
bleeding because a pH of 7 is required for optimal platelet func-
tion and clot formation.

ii. Definitive therapy
a) Sengstaken-Blakemore is an orogastric tube equipped with inflat-
able gastric and esophageal balloons. Inflation of the balloons applies
direct pressure to the bleeding varices and is useful for acute hemor-
rhage control until definitive therapy is available.

1) Endotracheal intubation should be performed prior to
Sengstaken-Blakemore tube placement.

2) Sengstaken-Blakemore tube placement controls hemorrhage in
85% of patients but is associated with a 65% re-bleed rate as well
as a 30% incidence of significant complications including sepsis
and esophageal perforation.

b) Endoscopic variceal ligation has replaced traditional sclerotherapy.

Bands are placed during a second endoscopy after diagnostic esopha-
gogastroduodenoscopy reveals bleeding varices and is successful
98% of the time. After endoscopic variceal ligation, repeat banding
at monthly intervals may be necessary to eradicate varices at risk for
bleeding.

sure with the portal pressure gradient from the portal vein to the

hepatic vein reduced to less than 12 mm Hg. Although contraindi-

it carries a significantly lower mortality that that of portosystemic

are rarely performed in the United States today.

2. Mallory-Weiss tears
a. Discussion. Mallory-Weiss tears are partial-thickness lacerations of the gas-
troesophageal junction that result from forceful emesis.

b. Clinical features. There is a history of vomiting in 85% of patients. Many
patients also have a history of alcohol abuse.

c. Assess hemodynamics. It is essential to obtain hemoglobin level and assess
magnitude of bleeding by measuring hemodynamic and orthostatic parameters.

d. Therapy. Bleeding usually stops spontaneously. When necessary, the condi-
tion can be treated with endoscopic clipping. Intravenous octreotide and in-
travenous PPI until urgent endoscopy is performed may be helpful.

e. Disposition. Many patients can be discharged after urgent endoscopy is per-
formed.
```
